Illinois data on violent crime and prison population

Violent crime in Illinois

In 2021, 328 of 934 law enforcement agencies in Illinois reported crime data to the FBI, covering 62 percent of the state’s population. This was too few agencies to make reliable statewide esimates of violent crime in 2021.

A list of law enforcement agencies in Illinois that did not fully report to the FBI on crime in 2021 can be found below.

  • All violent crime
  • Homicide
  • Aggravated assault
  • Robbery
  • Rape
  • By offense
Background on crime data reporting

Beginning with data year 2021, the FBI discontinued collecting Uniform Crime Reporting data via the Summary Reporting System and required law enforcement agencies to use the National Incident-Based Reporting System (NIBRS) to report crime statistics to the FBI. Because many states did not have full NIBRS reporting coverage in 2021, the FBI and Bureau of Justice Statistics generated estimated crime statistics based on the agencies that reported data to the FBI. The reported crime and clearance rates for 2021 have a level of uncertainty represented by a margin of error for each estimate because not all agencies reported data to the FBI.

Prison population in Illinois

There were 19,087 people in prison for violent offenses in 2020, comprising 65 percent of the total prison population. Since 2010, the number of people in prison for violent offenses decreased by 13 percent. During that same period, the number of people in prison for nonviolent offenses decreased by 60 percent. Data on prison population trends in 2021 and 2022 are not available in national datasets, but can be analyzed upon request.

  • Violent vs. nonviolent
  • By offense

Law enforcement agencies not reporting to NIBRS

Not all law enforcement agencies have begun reporting NIBRS data to the FBI. As of data year 2021, 328 of 934 agencies in Illinois reported NIBRS data, covering 62 percent of the state’s population. Agencies that did not report in 2021 are listed in the table below. The population column is the number of residents of the jurisdiction covered by the law enforcement agency.1

Data sources

Crime and clearance rates

Data regarding crime and clearance rates come from the FBI’s Uniform Crime Reporting Program. State-level counts of reported violent crime between 2010 and 2020 are derived from the Summary Reporting System (SRS) in which law enforcement agencies submitted monthly counts of the four violent Part 1 Index Crimes (murder, aggravated assault, rape, and robbery). The FBI generated state-level estimates for reported crime from 1979 to 2020 based on agency-level reports, and these estimates are used on this page for counts of crime. These estimates can be downloaded from the FBI’s Crime Data Explorer.

State-level crime rates are calculated by dividing the number of estimated offenses by the total state population. Population data comes from the U.S. Census Bureau American Community Survey 1-year estimates (table B01003) and the 2020 Decennial Census Redistricting Data (table P1).

State-level clearance rates are estimated by aggregating agency-level data from the SRS Offenses Known and Clearances by Arrest (Return A) data set. The clearance rates reported here are the number of offenses cleared by arrest plus the number of offenses exceptionally cleared divided by the total number of offenses known.

National rates of crime and clearances come from the FBI’s annual report, Crime in U.S.

State-level crime and clearance data for 2021 come from the FBI’s National Incident-Based Reporting System state estimation program. These estimates can be downloaded from the FBI’s Crime Data Explorer. Crime data for 2021 is included in these pages for states in which law enforcement agencies covering at least 90 percent of the population reported NIBRS data.

NIBRS participation

The number of agencies reporting to NIBRS comes from the the State level UCR Participation Endpoint API. The participation status of individual agencies comes from the Agency level UCR Participation Endpoint API. Documentation of the FBI crime data API endpoints can be found on Crime Data Explorer.

Prison population

State-level prison populations come from the Bureau of Justice Statistics National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP). NCRP data is collected annually from state departments of correction, which report individual-level data on people in prison custody. Not all states report data to BJS each year.

To generate the counts of people in prison, annual NCRP data was filtered to remove people with prison sentences of less than one year. An offense is associated with each person’s prison term and this offense was used to calculate prison population by offense. The violent offense category includes murder, manslaughter, rape, robbery, aggravated or simple assault, and other violent offenses.

Alaska, Connecticut, Delaware, Hawaii, Rhode Island, and Vermont have integrated systems that combine prisons and jails, so the populations reported to NCRP include people held in both prisons and jails.

NCRP data can be downloaded from the National Archive of Criminal Justice Data.

Footnotes

  1. Some agencies, such as university and park police, are considered to have a population of zero by the FBI.↩︎
